meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Resultant Force by Surface on Block

intro.kicker: Topic 8 of 38 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Resultant Force by Surface on Block
intro.description: When a block rests on a surface, the surface exerts two perpendicular force components on it: the normal reaction R (perpendicular to surface) and the friction force F (parallel to surface). The resultant contact force S = √(F² + R²). At zero friction (smooth surface) S = mg (minimum). At limiting friction S = mg√(μ²+1) (maximum). NEET tests the range mg ≤ S ≤ mg√(μ²+1) and the vector nature of the contact force from surfaces.

pills.text: Vector sum of N and f

expectedQuestions.description: Appears in NEET as a direct formula application or conceptual MCQ. Typical patterns: (i) compute S given m, μ and g; (ii) state the minimum value of S (= mg when surface is smooth); (iii) identify which expression gives the maximum contact force. One question every 2–3 years.

timeRequired.description: 20 min theory (vector derivation + range formula), 10 min targeted MCQ practice. The derivation is short but essential for NEET: understand why the minimum is mg (no friction) and why the maximum is mg√(μ²+1) (at limiting friction). The range expression is the most tested form.

difficulty.description: Straightforward once the vector diagram is mastered. The key trap: students confuse S with the normal reaction R. S is the total contact force (resultant), whereas R = mg (for a horizontal surface). The formula S = √(F² + R²) is simply Pythagoras applied to the two perpendicular surface force components.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics covers normal and friction forces separately but rarely tests their vector resultant. NEET explicitly tests the range formula mg ≤ S ≤ mg√(μ²+1) as a standalone MCQ item. Students from US curricula should practise computing S as a Pythagorean resultant and memorise the two extreme values.

weightage.sectionTitle: Resultant Force by Surface on Block — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Appears once every 2–3 years; targeted formula MCQ type.
Minimum contact force (smooth surface) = mg is a direct concept question.
Maximum contact force formula S = mg√(μ²+1) is tested via numerical substitution.

strategySteps.title: Derive S from Pythagoras
strategySteps.description: S = √(F² + R²). Substitute F = μR = μmg and R = mg: S = √((μmg)²+(mg)²) = mg√(μ²+1). This derivation is the only one needed for NEET.

strategySteps.title: Memorise the Range
strategySteps.description: When μ = 0: S = mg (minimum). At limiting friction: S = mg√(μ²+1) (maximum). Range: mg ≤ S ≤ mg√(μ²+1). NEET may ask for either extreme.

strategySteps.title: Distinguish S from R and F
strategySteps.description: R = mg (normal reaction, vertical for horizontal surface). F ≤ μmg (friction, horizontal). S = vector sum of R and F. Never write S = R + F (scalar addition is wrong for perpendicular vectors).

cards.title: Resultant Contact Force Notes
cards.description: Complete derivation of S = mg√(μ²+1), the range formula, vector diagram, and 5 solved NEET-pattern MCQs on contact force magnitude.

cards.title: Contact Force Quick-Reference Card
cards.description: One-page formula card: S = mg√(μ²+1), range inequality, angle of resultant with normal, and common numerical values for standard μ.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Resultant Force by Surface on Block

cards.title: Net Contact Force Formula
cards.coreRevision: The surface exerts a normal reaction R perpendicular to itself and a friction force F parallel to itself. Their vector resultant is S = √(F² + R²). For a block on a horizontal surface, R = mg and at limiting friction F = μmg, giving S = mg√(μ²+1). The range is mg ≤ S ≤ mg√(μ²+1).
points: S = √(F² + R²) — Pythagorean magnitude of contact force
Minimum S = mg when μ = 0 (frictionless surface)
Maximum S = mg√(μ²+1) at limiting friction
S is always ≥ mg for any surface with friction
Angle of S with normal = angle of friction θ = tan⁻¹(μ)
cards.example: Block of mass 2 kg, μ = 0.75, g = 10 m/s². At limiting friction: S = 2×10×√(0.75²+1) = 20√(0.5625+1) = 20√1.5625 = 20×1.25 = 25 N. Minimum S (smooth surface) = 2×10 = 20 N.

cards.title: Range of Contact Force
cards.coreRevision: The contact force S cannot be less than mg (the weight supporting component) because the normal reaction alone equals mg when there is no friction. As friction increases from 0 to its limiting value μmg, S increases from mg to mg√(μ²+1). This range is a direct NEET conceptual MCQ.
points: S is minimum for smooth surface: S_min = mg
S is maximum at limiting friction: S_max = mg√(μ²+1)
For rough surface in static equilibrium: mg ≤ S ≤ mg√(μ²+1)
The resultant S always makes angle θ ≤ angle of friction with normal
NEET trap: S ≠ mg + μmg (scalar sum); must use Pythagoras

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics and common US curricula compare to NEET scope on resultant contact force.

gaps.title: Resultant Contact Force Range Not Tested in AP Physics
gaps.description: AP Physics 1 and C treat normal force and friction force as separate vectors; the concept of a 'resultant contact force S' with a specific range formula is not tested. NEET Class 11 explicitly requires the range mg ≤ S ≤ mg√(μ²+1) to be memorised and applied in MCQs.
bullets: Learn S = √(N² + f²) as the contact force resultant.
Memorise both extreme values: S_min = mg and S_max = mg√(μ²+1).

gaps.title: Angle of Resultant with Normal
gaps.description: The resultant contact force S makes an angle θ = tan⁻¹(f/N) with the normal. This angle equals the angle of friction at limiting friction, and is less than the angle of friction when friction is below its limit. AP Physics does not emphasise this angular relationship.
bullets: At limiting friction: angle of S with normal = angle of friction = tan⁻¹(μ).
This geometric link between S and angle of friction is an NEET-targeted concept.

questions.question: A block of mass 5 kg is placed on a rough horizontal surface with coefficient of friction μ = 0.4. The resultant contact force exerted by the surface on the block is (g = 10 m/s²):
For a block at rest on a rough horizontal surface, the minimum possible value of the resultant contact force exerted by the surface is:
questions.explanation: The resultant contact force S = √(F²+R²). When friction is zero (μ=0), F=0 and R=mg, so S_min = mg. As friction increases, S increases. Hence the minimum is mg (frictionless surface), and S can never be less than mg for a block in vertical equilibrium.

questions.question: A 10 kg block rests on a rough surface with μ = 0.5. The maximum resultant contact force exerted by the surface is (g = 10 m/s²):
The resultant contact force on a block resting on a surface with no friction equals:
For a block on a rough surface, if the applied force equals limiting friction, which formula gives the resultant contact force?
The angle which the resultant contact force makes with the normal to the surface at limiting friction equals:

items.title: State the resultant contact force formula
items.description: S = √(F² + R²) = mg√(μ²+1) at limiting friction. Both forms must be recalled promptly.

items.title: State the range of S
items.description: mg ≤ S ≤ mg√(μ²+1). Know which value corresponds to smooth (μ=0) and which to rough (limiting friction) surface.

items.title: Verify S² = F² + R²
items.description: Never add F and R as scalars. They are perpendicular vectors, so S² = F² + R². Scalar sum F+R is a common NEET trap.

items.title: Link to angle of friction
items.description: At limiting friction, the resultant S makes exactly angle θ = tan⁻¹(μ) with the normal. This is the definition of the angle of friction — connect the two concepts.

items.question: Why is the minimum contact force equal to mg and not zero?
items.answer: The block is in vertical equilibrium on the surface. The surface must support the block's weight, so the normal reaction R = mg is always present. Even with zero friction (smooth surface), the surface still pushes up with force mg. Hence S = √(0² + mg²) = mg is the minimum. S = 0 would mean the block is floating, which contradicts contact.

items.question: What is the difference between the contact force S and the normal reaction R?
items.answer: The normal reaction R is only the component of the contact force perpendicular to the surface. The contact force S = √(R² + F²) is the total (vector sum of R and friction F). For a vertical smooth surface, R can equal S because friction is zero in that direction. For rough horizontal surfaces, S > R always when friction is present.

items.question: At what condition does S equal mg√(μ²+1)?
items.answer: S = mg√(μ²+1) when friction acts at its limiting (maximum) value F = μmg. This is when the block is on the verge of sliding. Below limiting friction, F < μmg and hence S is between mg and mg√(μ²+1).

items.question: Does the contact force depend on the applied external force?
items.answer: Yes. As an applied force is increased from zero to limiting friction, the friction component F increases (self-adjusting), so S = √(F² + R²) increases from mg to mg√(μ²+1). Once motion starts Fk = μk R is fixed, so S stabilises at mg√(μk²+1). The contact force is not constant unless friction has reached its limit.

grounding_summary: Page 238 (textbook p. 225) derives the resultant surface force S = mg√(μ²+1) using S = √(F² + R²) with F = μmg at limiting friction and R = mg. It establishes the range mg ≤ S ≤ mg√(μ²+1) with the lower bound at μ = 0.
The page explicitly states that when there is no friction, S is minimum and equals mg, confirming the lower bound of the range formula.
The angle of friction context on page 238 confirms the link: the resultant contact force makes the angle of friction with the normal at limiting friction, completing the geometric picture.
grounding_excerpt: Friction always opposes the relative motion between any two bodies in contact.
Hence coefficient of static friction is equal to tangent of the angle of friction.
Angle of repose is defined as the angle of the inclined plane with horizontal such that a body placed on it just begins to slide.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Friction Section)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source for friction laws, normal force, and contact force derivations aligned with NEET syllabus.

sources.title: NEET Physics Class 11 Chapter 6 Friction Notes — ClassNotes.guru
sources.why_used: Verified NEET-targeted notes confirming S = √(f²+N²) as the net contact force formula and NEET question patterns for friction.

review.notes: Content grounded on page-238 OCR. Resultant force range formula mg <= S <= mg*sqrt(mu^2+1) verified from textbook derivation.
